Key Takeaways:
o There are three phases to your prelaunch
o Pre-Prelaunch
▪ Typically, 1-4 weeks long - for me they’re usually 2 weeks
o Prelaunch
▪ Typically, 9-12 days long for most launches
▪ If it’s a big important product it could be longer for you - or vice
versa
▪ Whether you’re in a higher or lower end market is for you to decide
and there’s nothing wrong with either - just make sure you’re in the
higher end of your own market
▪ Many PLF Owners sell small ticket items ($10-100) and they do very
well - never apologize about your pricing
▪ A higher ticket item generally needs more education and more time
to make the sale
o • Open Cart
▪ Typically, 5-7 days - you can go shorter
▪ I’ve had Open Cart periods as short as 1.5 days
▪ Don’t go too short on your very first launch
▪ A longer period gives you more time to tweak your mistakes as
needed
▪ Remember there’s a “Product Launch Parachute” - lots of
interactions throughout the process
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 6 --

3
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
▪ If something isn’t perfect, you have time to recover
▪ Shorter open cart periods are dangerous
▪ Longer than 7 days can turn into a “begathon”
▪ You should be emailing your launch list every day but after 7 days
you start to run out of things to say
o PLC #1 is released on the first day of your prelaunch
o PLC #2 is typically on day 4
o PLC #3 is typically day 8
o Day 10 you’ll open cart
o This is just the general timing but it can vary. From the start of prelaunch to the
end when you close cart (closing your launch offer) is from 14-19 days
o This is very important: You need to close cart by ending with something
“negative”
o Examples: close cart, bonuses go away, price goes up - but there needs to
be a definitive end to the launch
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Okay, so now I want to talk about the timing of the Prelaunch.
Lots and lots of questions about this, I always get lots of questions. It’s only
natural because you see the sequences and want to know how they all fit together.
To be clear, this is not to scale. I’m going to split it. It’s basically three main
periods here.
In the middle we have the Prelaunch. You’ve heard me talk about Prelaunch.
That’s the sideways sales letter where you release your three pieces of Prelaunch
Content, you’ve got people all excited about your launch. That’s the Prelaunch.
Then we come into the Open Cart. That’s where you actually open up and start
taking orders, that's the Open Cart.
We have a Prelaunch period and Open Cart period.
The one you haven’t heard me talk too much about is what we call the Pre-
Prelaunch. The Pre-Prelaunch could be the longest of all these, so the Pre-
Prelaunch can last anywhere from 1 to 4 weeks long.
They’re usually about 2 weeks long for me. They’re a lot less defined than these 2
periods. I’m actually going to be talking about the Pre-Prelaunch in this module and
walking you through some examples.
A Pre-Prelaunch can be very, very cool. It’s something you don’t have to do, but
once you start using some type of a Pre-Prelaunch then you’ll realize how cool they are
and then you’ll start to think about them more often.
Then the Prelaunch period is typically 9 to 12 days long in most
launches. It could be longer, could be shorter.
Now typically bigger-ticket items, or if you come out with a brand new flagship
product, a really big important launch for you, it’s generally going to be longer.
If it’s more of a quicker, a lower-priced product for you, then it could be shorter.
Of course, pricing is completely relative to whatever market you’re in.
If you’re teaching Fortune 500 executives how to present themselves on stage,
okay, that’s probably a really high-ticket item, high-dollar item. If you’re teaching people
how to decorate cakes, probably not going to be as high-priced.
When I’m talking about whether something’s high-priced or low-priced, it’s
relative to your market. I don’t know what cake decorating products go for, but maybe at
the low end they’re $10, at the high end maybe they’re $50.
The thing is, with those executives, teaching those Fortune 500 executives how to
present, you’re probably talking more like $2000 might be the low end and $10,000
might be the high end. Again, when I’m talking about higher-ticket items and lower-
ticket items, it’s relative to your market.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 6 --

5
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
You never have to apologize to me if you have a lower-end market. I’ve got plenty
of PLF owners who sell lots and lots of products that are not $1000, $2000 or even
$5000.
They might be selling $10 products or $100 products and selling tons of them
and they’ve got a great business. Don’t ever apologize to me or anyone else about your
pricing. The key is, is I think your using these type of strategies, you should be at the high
end of whatever your market is so it’s completely relative to what other people are selling
in your market.
In any case, typically a higher-ticket item’s going to have a longer
Prelaunch and a lower-ticket item’s going to have a shorter Prelaunch. You
need a lot less copy, a lot less marketing to sell a gallon of milk compared to selling a
Lamborghini, right? It just makes sense. A higher-ticket sale is a more involved sale,
generally. It needs more education and just more communication.
Most Prelaunch periods, 9 to 12 days and then the Open Cart period
is typically 5 to 7 days.
Now you can go shorter. I’ve had Open Cart periods for my launches that were
like a day-and-a-half, okay? The thing is, if you’re going to go really short, probably not a
good thing to do on your very first launch because if something isn't perfect, it doesn’t
give you time to recover. A longer cart period gives you more time just to tweak your
message and recover from any mistakes you make during your Open Cart.
Remember there’s that product launch parachute. The product launch
parachute means that there’s lots and lots of interactions throughout this entire process
so if something isn’t perfect, you have time to recover.
You’re not depending on one email or one video or one anything. Those really
short open-cart periods, they’re a little on the dangerous side and when I did them, well,
I’m a trained professional. I’ve done dozens and dozens and dozens of these launches so I
felt comfortable doing it.
Now on the other hand, go beyond 7 days and it will often turn into a beg-a-thon,
because when your cart is open, you’re going to want to be emailing your launch list
every single day.
Frankly, after 7 days you start to run out of things to say for most launches. Later
on I’ll get into what you actually do during that Open Cart period.
I think for most people, 5 to 7 days is that golden period. Let’s just blow this out a
little bit...
o Typically, on day 1 of your Prelaunch period is your first piece of PLC.
This is just classic. All this stuff’s very, very flexible and you can change
stuff around.
o PLC number 2 would typically be on day 4. You release your Prelaunch
Content then on day 1. Then day 2, day 3, you let people digest it. Then
day 4 will be your second piece of Prelaunch Content,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 6 --

6
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
o and then on day 8, will be PLC number 3.
o Then you’ll Open Cart on day 10. This is day 10 and you’re going to open.
This can vary quite a bit. (Then of course from the Open Cart you go
forward 5 days to the Close Cart.)
You can go day 1, you could go day 3, you could go day 7, you could go day 9, no
problem, but that’s typically how it will go.
The key here is you want to give your people a little bit of time to digest each
piece of content as you move forward. That’s just general timing, and then of course from
the Open Cart you go forward 5 days.
From the start of Prelaunch to when you close cart will typically be anywhere
from 14 to 19 days. In other words, if you wanted the short end here, you want 9 days
Prelaunch and 5 days Open Cart, that would give you 14 days from the start of Prelaunch
to the end, to the closing cart.
If you wanted a 12-day Prelaunch and you kept the cart open for 7 days, that’s 19
days from the start of Prelaunch to the end of the launch when you close the cart.
When I say close cart, that’s one little asterisk. We’ll get to this in more detail
later on, but when I say close cart, that means you’re closing your launch
offer. At the end of every launch, there has to be some negative consequence if people
don’t buy from you.
One silly way I put it is something bad has to happen if they don’t take action, but
that doesn’t mean you have to pull the product off the market.
Lots of times I do pull the product off the market because of the nature of the way
I teach Product Launch Formula. Just because I do that doesn’t mean you have to do it.
There’s lots of other ways to basically put that definitive end on your launch...
o you can close the cart, meaning that people can’t buy from you
o you can raise the price
o bonuses can go away
There’s different ways to do it, but you have to put that definitive end on your
launch or else there’s going to be a lot of people that are sitting on the fence that don’t
take action unless you hold their feet to the fire and say, “This is when you have to take
action.”
That’s the timing of the overall Prelaunch, 1 to 4 weeks long, 9 to 12 days, and 5 to
7 days, and now we’re going to get into the Pre-Prelaunch.
